coredata.dc:title: Minute-level synthesis of tunable carbon dots-based phosphorescence materials for information encryption and light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zihan
Zhou, Jiarui
Zhang, Runzhi
Wang, Xin
Wei, Hua
Sun, Xinyu
Hu, Wei
Zhao, Lei
Zhou, Mingyang
Yang, Shenghong
coredata.dc:description: The emergence of carbon dots has broadened the design and application of new room-temperature phosphorescence materials, but the mass production of long-lived color-tunable carbon dots-based phosphorescence materials (CDPMs) is still hindered by solvent dependence and time-consuming. In this work, a minute-level solid-phase synthesis strategy was proposed for the preparation of a series of CDPMs through pyrolyzing the mixture of boric acid and pimelic acid. By tuning the mass ratio of boric acid and pimelic acid, CDPMs with different room-temperature phosphorescence characterises can be obtained within 7 min. Preferably, the CDPMs-60 synthesized from the mass ratio of boric acid and pimelic acid of 60:1 exhibits a long afterglow length up to 10 s and has obvious phosphorescence chromatic aberrations following excitation at 254 and 365 nm, and phosphorescence lifetimes of CDPMs-60 in the blue and green band can reach 1030 ms and 555 ms, respectively. The CDPMs were further successfully applied for the information encryption and fabricating light emitting diodes based on their simple synthesis process, exceptional optical properties and low-toxicity. This study may provide a valuable reference for the facile synthesis and suitable commercial application of room temperature phosphorescence materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Minute-level solid phase synthesis
Carbon dots
Room temperature phosphorescence materials
Information encryption
Light emitting diodes

ZR2022MC153 , ZR2023MA089 ), the Program of New Collegiate 20 Items in Jinan (Nos. 2021GXRC042 , 202228031 ) the Science, Education and Industry Integration Innovation Pilot Project from Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Nos. 2022JBZ02-04 , 2022PY073 , 2022PY061 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The afterglow images of CDPMs under 254 nm (A) and 365 nm (B) excitation light. Fluorescence (C, E) and phosphorescence spectra (D, F) of CDPMs-60. The phosphorescence decay spectra of CDPMs-60 with emission wavelengths of 425 nm (G) and 525 nm (H).

The XRD pattern (A), TEM image (B), high-resolution TEM images (C) of CDPMs-60, the high-resolution XPS spectra of B 1s (D), C 1s (E) and O1s (F) of CDPMs-60. Inset: the corresponding lattice fringes of CDPMs-60.
The phosphorescence decay spectra of CDPMs-60 with emission wavelengths of 425 nm (A) and 525 nm (B). Schematic of the emission processes of proposed CDPMs (C).
Formula encryption (A), image security (B), and pattern information encryption (C) applications of CDPMs.

The fluorescence (A) and phosphorescence (B) spectra of CDPMs-60 under the excitation wavelength at 365 nm, CIE color coordinate of the LEDs based on CDPMs-60 (C). The phosphorescence LED images of CDPMs-60 after cutting off the power (D).
Illustrating the minute-level solid-phase synthesis strategy and optical property tunability of the proposed series of CDPMs.

head.ce:title: Minute-level synthesis of tunable carbon dots-based phosphorescence materials for information encryption and light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao, 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emergence of carbon dots has broadened the design and application of new room-temperature phosphorescence materials, but the mass production of long-lived color-tunable carbon dots-based phosphorescence materials (CDPMs) is still hindered by solvent dependence and time-consuming. In this work, a minute-level solid-phase synthesis strategy was proposed for the preparation of a series of CDPMs through pyrolyzing the mixture of boric acid and pimelic acid. By tuning the mass ratio of boric acid and pimelic acid, CDPMs with different room-temperature phosphorescence characterises can be obtained within 7 min. Preferably, the CDPMs-60 synthesized from the mass ratio of boric acid and pimelic acid of 60:1 exhibits a long afterglow length up to 10 s and has obvious phosphorescence chromatic aberrations following excitation at 254 and 365 nm, and phosphorescence lifetimes of CDPMs-60 in the blue and green band can reach 1030 ms and 555 ms, respectively. The CDPMs were further successfully applied for the information encryption and fabricating light emitting diodes based on their simple synthesis process, exceptional optical properties and low-toxicity. This study may provide a valuable reference for the facile synthesis and suitable commercial application of room temperature phosphorescence materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with unique afterglow luminescence characteristics have exhibited extensive application prospects in information encryption [1–3], low-interference biomedical sensing [4–6], decoration and display [7], and so on. In particular, RTP materials have an afterglow lasting for seconds after the excitation light source is removed, which also has received considerable attention in emergency signals [8]. The generation of high-efficiency RTP mainly depends on the difficulty of intersystem crossing from the singlet state (S1) to the triplet state (T1) with a spin-forbidden process and the sensitivity of T1 to environmental factors such as water and oxygen [9–11]. To obtain RTP materials with long afterglow length, commonly, metal and other heteroatom doped and introducing carbonyl functional groups were adopted to decrease the barrier of the spin-forbidden process while constructing rigid environments to prevent the T1 quenching through nonradiative relaxation channel [12]. Proceeding from the above mechanism, up to now, many RTP materials have been reported and mainly focused on pure organics [13,14], organometallic complexes [15], and rare-earth metal compounds [16,17]. However, the practical application of these RTP materials is still limited by high-cost, complicated preparation procedures and respective inherent defects. The luminescence lifetimes of pure organics are short with only a few milliseconds, and the metal-containing RTP materials cannot avoid the biological toxicity effect from metal atoms [18]. Thus, the development of novel types of RTP materials with facile and simple synthesis route, cost-effective, low-toxicity and superior afterglow is still highly advocated.

Carbon dots (CDs) have aroused much more attention and experienced more than ten years of deep research since it was first found in 2004 [19–21]. Different from other types of fluorescent nanomaterials, CDs were initially advocated in bioimaging and chemo-/bio-sensing fields benefit by both good biocompatibility and fluorescent properties [22–26]. The heteroatom doping strategy was preferred to improve the fluorescence efficiency and adjust the luminescent center of CDs and endow new characters of catalysis and magnetism, which further broad the application fields of CDs in optoelectronic devices [27–29], pollutant control [30–32], and magnetic imaging [33–35]. Except for common fluorescent properties, CDs also exhibits many new optical characteristics in recent years, such as near-infrared absorption, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, up-conversion luminescence, electrochemical luminescence, and RTP [36–40]. In particular, CDs with RTP characteristics can effectively overcome the shortcomings of traditional phosphorescent materials in high toxicity and short afterglow length, which provides a superior alternative material for the optical elements and information encryption applications [41–43].

Some efforts have been performed to construct CDs-based phosphorescence materials (CDPMs) and promote their performance to date [9,44,45]. He et al. anchored CDs onto amorphous nano-silica to improve the intersystem crossing and enhance the RTP intensity and luminous lifetime, which provides a valuable mind for the synthesis of CDPMs [46]. Furthermore, polymers, such as polyacrylamide, polyvinylpyrrolidone, etc., also were selected as the matrix to construct composites with CDs and stabilize triplet-excited state through immobilizing emissive species and ensuring the generation and retention of RTP [47,48]. However, the preparation of these composites usually needs two steps: CDs synthesis and substrate loading, which still suffers from a complicated synthesis process [49,50]. Hence, a one-step pyrolysis route was advocated to simplify the procedures and promote mass production. Typically, Ding et al. fabricated boron-doped CDs composites with excellent RTP performance by pyrolyzing the mixture of citric acid and boric acid [51]. In the one-pot reaction, the generation and loading of CDs is along with the formation of B2O3 matrix, greatly simplifying the operation steps. Unfortunately, the real application of these preparation routes in large-scale fabrication is still limited by solvent dependence and time-consuming (more than 3h). Besides, it is still difficult to precisely tune the phosphorescence performance within a single system and realize controllable afterglow color by changing the excitation light. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a facile and rapid synthesis method for synthesizing CDPMs with tunable RTP properties to realize reasonable and extensive applications of CDPMs.

Given the advantages in optical properties and biocompatibility of CDPMs and their synthetic methodology deficiency, in this study, we proposed a minute-level solid-phase synthesis strategy for synthesizing a series of color-tunable CDPMs just through microwave-assisted pyrolyzing the mixture of boric acid (BA) and pimelic acid (PA) for as short as 7 min (Scheme 1 ). The RTP color and afterglow length of CDPMs can be tuned flexibly just by changing the mass ratio of BA and PA. Particularly, the CDPMs-60 obtained from the mass ratio of 60:1 exhibits fascinating RTP characteristics, the CDPMs-60 can emit blue/blue-white fluorescence under 254 nm and 365 nm UV light irradiation, while the RTP was changed to blue and green light after 254 nm and 365 nm light was turned off, respectively. The RTP lifetime of CDPMs-60 can reach 1.0 s with a long-lived afterglow that is visible to the naked eye for 10 s. Based on the special color-tunable afterglow property, proposed series of CDPMs were successfully applied for time-resolved anticounterfeiting and information encryption. The application feasibility of CDPMs in the optoelectronic field was verified by fabricating light-emitting diodes (LEDs) by using CDPMs-60 as light conversion layer. This study may provide a valuable reference for the emergency synthesis of RTP materials, and can further promote the practical application of CDPMs.
Boric acid (BA), Pimelic acid (PA), and other organic acids were all purchased from Aladdin Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) without extra purification.

Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDPMs were recorded by an F97Pro fluorescent spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The morphology of CDPMs-60 was characterized by a Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a 200 kV accelerating voltage (FEI, Oregon, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, D8-ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany) was used to characterize the crystal structure of CDPMs-60. The surface structure and elemental composition of CDPMs-60 were analyzed by EscaLab Xi + X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and NEXUS 670 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet, USA). The quantum yields of CDPMs-60 were measured by Steady State and Transient State Fluorescence Spectrometer (FLS-100, Edinburgh, UK) and calculated by peak-differentiation-imitating analysis. The toxicity evaluation of CDPMs-60 was performed through an MTT assay using an RT-6100 enzyme-mark analyzer (Shenzhen, China).

The CDPMs were prepared through a rapid microwave-assisted solid-phase synthesis method. Typically, 3 g BA and different amounts of PA were transferred into an agate mortar, and the mixture was ground to a uniform powder. The obtained powder was transferred to a beaker and heated in a domestic microwave oven (800 W) for 7 min, the products were collected and further ground for subsequent experiments.

Ultraviolet LEDs chips (365 nm) with a luminous efficiency of 1.44 lm·W−1 were obtained from Yuanyue Photoelectric Technology Co., Ltd., (Shenzhen, China). The 365 nm LEDs chip and CDPMs-60 were used as the base and phosphorescence powder, respectively, and the working voltage was set at 3 V. The CDPMs-60 was dispersed uniformly in an epoxy hardener, and the obtained solution was mixed with a hardener at a 4:1 vol ratio. Then, the mixture was coated onto the LEDs chips and cured for 1 h at 100 °C, CDPMs-based LEDs were successfully fabricated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and property modulation of CDPMs
ce:section.ce:para: A series of CDPMs with varying optical properties were synthesized easily just by adjusting the mass ratio of BA and PA through the microwave-assisted solid phase pyrolysis method. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the fluorescence and phosphorescence colors of CDPMs gradually transition from blue-white to yellow when excited at 254 nm as the mass ratios change from 300:1 to 2:1. In Fig. 1B, the fluorescence color change under 365 nm excitation is like 254 nm excitation, while the phosphorescence colors can transition from green to yellow-green. The CDPMs obtained from the mass ratios of 150:1 and 60:1 exhibits superior luminous intensity and afterglow duration that can exceed 10.0 s and visible to the naked eye compared to those obtained using other ratios (Fig. S1). The excellent RTP of CDPMs should be attributed to the spatial restriction of the B2O3 polycrystalline network to CDs [51]. At a high ratio of BA and PA, the formed CDs-based luminous centers are insufficient compared with the B2O3 obtained from BA, leading to a lower luminous intensity. On the other hand, the over carbonization of CDs can further cause the emission wavelength red-shift and luminous intensity decrease of obtained CDPMs with increasing the amounts of PA. Thus, the CDPMs synthesized from the mass ratio of BA and PA at 150:1 and 60:1 can maximize the synergistic effect of B2O3 and CDs, exhibiting superior RTP performance. Furthermore, the effect of reaction time on CDPMs performance also was investigated, and the obtained CDPMs with reacting for 7 min showed superior afterglow length and intensity as shown in Fig.S2. Therefore, the CDPMs-60 synthesized with a mass ratio of BA and PA of 60:1 and the reaction time of 7 min was chosen for further characterization. When excited at 254 nm, the fluorescence spectrum of CDPMs-60 displays a broad emission band, while its afterglow spectrum shows a typical peak at 410 nm (Fig. 1C, D). In contrast, when the excitation light was turned on and off at 365 nm, the emission peaks of CDPMs were observed at 436 nm and 508 nm, respectively (Fig. 1E, F). As shown in Fig.S3, the phosphorescence emission of CDPMs-60 gradually red-shift with the increasement of excitation wavelength from 210 nm to 410 nm, which indicates the phosphorescence of CDPM-60 can be excitated by a wide wavelength range. Furthermore, as shown in Fig.S4, the BA also can react with other organic acids to form CDPMs with various RTP properties, such as ethanedioic acid, propanedioic acid, succinic acid, glutaric acid, adipic acid, aspartic acid, malic acid, etc., which further proves the proposed synthesis route also has a universality for constructing CDPMs rapidly.

The morphology and surface structure of CDPMs-60 was characterized systemically. The XRD pattern shows an obvious B2O3 characteristic peak, indicating the presence of a B2O3 matrix [32] (Fig. 2 A). In Fig. 2B, CDs are uniformly dispersed within the B2O3 matrix. The CDs exhibit a distinct crystal structure with lattice fringes spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite [52] (Fig. 2C). The full XPS spectrum suggests the existence of B, C, and O elements (Fig. S5). The high-resolution B 1s spectrum reveals the presence of B–CO2 (192.88 eV), B2O3 (193.87 eV), and B–O (194.88 eV) (Fig. 2D). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s proves the existence of C–C/CC (286.88 eV), C–O/C–O–B (287.78 eV), CO (288.68 eV), and O–CO (289.48 eV) (Fig. 2E), while that of O 1 s displays the presence of C–O (533.58 eV), and CO (532.28 eV) (Fig. 2F). The FT-IR spectrum reveals multiple vibration peaks in CDPMs-60, including –OH (3187.2 cm−1), B–O (1426.0 cm−1), B–O–H (1192.2 cm−1), B–C (904.4 cm−1) (Fig. S6). All the above results indicate the formation of the B2O3 matrix and CDs and the presence of abundant surface functional groups in CDPMs-60. From Fig. S7, we can see that only CDPMs-60 can exhibit perfect RTP signal, and the products obtained by single pyrolyzing BA or PA can emit non/weak phosphorescence. To further understand the characteristics of CDPMs-60, we also measure their phosphorescence decay spectrum. The afterglow lifetimes of the CDPMs-60 at the emission wavelength of 425 nm and 525 nm are 1030 ms and 555 ms, respectively and the phosphorescence quantum yields of that are 6.53% and 4.01%, according to Eq (S1) (Fig. 3A and B). The CDPMs-60 powder shows excitation-dependent RTP behavior with two luminous bands of blue and green, inferring the existence of two distinct phosphorescence bands in the CDPMs-60 (Fig. 3 C). The energy gap of CDPMs-60 was calculated in the range of 0.18–0.39 eV, which is more favourable to the generation of RTP through promoting the conversion of S1 to T1 [33]. The ultra-long RTP of CDPMs-60 should be attributed to the spatial restriction of the B2O3 polycrystalline network to CDs like the previous report [51]. The obvious advantage of this study is the simultaneous formation of matrix and CDs in a minute-level reaction time compared with other reports [51,53]. Besides, from the MTT results (Fig. S8), we can see that the CDPMs-60 also has good biocompatibility, which guarantees the security application of CDPMs.

Given the perfect RTP properties, proposed series of CDPMs were successfully applied for the image security and information encryption. Firstly, two types of CDPMs with different afterglow lengths were selected for digital encryption (the mass ratios of BA and PA are 30:1 and 5:1). In Fig. 4 A, the formula of "6 + 2 = 8″ can emit obvious fluorescence when excited by UV light, but it would change to "1 + 2 = 3″ after removing the UV light. Furthermore, two CDPMs with different RTP colors and duration times (the mass ratios of BA and PA are 60:1 and 5:1) were used to demonstrate the feasibility of proposed materials in image security. In Fig. 4B, we can see that the outer edge and core of a flower pattern can emit different fluorescence under the irradiation of UV light or the initial stage of UV light shutdown. After a few seconds, the core of a flower luminescence disappeared while petal luminescence remained based on the afterglow length differences. Furthermore, the CDPMs of the petal after 254 nm and 365 nm of irradiation exhibit obvious blue and green light, respectively. More interestingly, the RTP characteristic of CDPMs would disappear upon exposure to water, which may be attributed to the structural collapse of CDPMs caused by the hydrolysis of B2O3 into boric acid [32]. Based on this, we further applied information encryption by constructing an "888″ pattern using CDPMs. The CDPMs were poured into the mold of "888″, and water was introduced to the central section. As shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence pattern of "888″ can be recognized easily under UV light excitation, while a distinct pattern of "000″ appeared when UV light was turned off. All the above demonstrations indicate that the proposed CDPMs hold great application prospect in the fields of image security and information encryption.

Considering the stability and continuity of the optical signal and the good biocompatibility of CDPMs-60, CDPMs-60 obtained through a simple minute-level synthesis route was further applied for fabricating LEDs devices. In Fig. 5 A and B, the CDPM-60-based LEDs can emit blue-white light with the power on and display green afterglow after the power down, which is beneficial for construing low/no flicker lighting elements [8]. According to the emission spectra of the CDPMs-60, we calculated the fluorescence and phosphorescence CIE color coordinate of the LEDs as (0.19, 0.19) and (0.21, 0.49), respectively, indicating that the constructed LEDs have good luminescence and color rendering performance (Fig. 5C). Moreover, the light of CDPMs-based LEDs also can remain for several seconds after cutting off power (Fig. 5D). Besides, the CDPMs-based LEDs still possess high stability and can maintain good luminous performance after being stored for several months, which exhibits a potential prospect in the emergency lighting field.

In summary, we proposed a rapid and highly efficient microwave-assisted solid-phase synthesis route for the simple and mass production of color-tunable CDPMs just through pyrolyzing the mixture of BA and PA. The RTP color and afterglow length of CDPMs can be tuned easily by controlling the mass ratio of reactants, and the phosphorescence lifetime and afterglow time can reach up to 1030 ms and 10 s, respectively. The excellent RTP performance of the CDPMs should be attributed to the formation of a composite system of B2O3 matrix and CDs. Based on the tunable afterglow color and length, the developed CDPMs were successfully applied in image security, information encryption, and LEDs. This study may provide valuable insights into facile and large-scale production as well as reasonable applications of new CDs-based phosphorescent materials.

Zihan Li: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiarui Zhou: Data curation. Runzhi Zhang: Data curation. Xin Wang: Methodology. Hua Wei: Investigation. Xinyu Sun: Validation. Wei Hu: Funding acquisition. Lei Zhao: Funding acquisition. Mingyang Zhou: Funding acquisition. Shenghong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from the projects of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22104045), the Young Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province (tsqn201909139), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Nos. ZR2022MC153, ZR2023MA089), the Program of New Collegiate 20 Items in Jinan (Nos. 2021GXRC042, 202228031) the Science, Education and Industry Integration Innovation Pilot Project from Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Nos. 2022JBZ02-04, 2022PY073, 2022PY061).

Meng, Gram-scale synthesis of 41% efficient single-component white-light-emissive carbonized polymer dots with hybrid fluorescence/phosphorescence for white light-emitting diodes, Adv. Sci. 7 (2020) 1902688.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorene-based host-guest phosphorescence materials for information encryption

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Time-dependent phosphorescence colors from carbon dots for advanced dynamic information encryption

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence–phosphorescence dual emissive carbon nitride quantum dots show 25% white emission efficiency enabling single-component WLEDs

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous promotion of efficiency and lifetime of organic phosphorescence for self-referenced temperature sensing

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-powered persistent phosphorescence for reliable optical display

Yu, One-pot three carbon dots with various lifetimes rooted in different decarboxylation degrees for matrix-free, anti-oxygen, and time-resolved information encryption and cellular Imaging. Anal. Chem. 95 (2023) 1985-1994.
